course_description:
The primary aim of this course is to examine how biological processes are studied at a molecular level. The emphasis is on the experimental approaches that are used to study proteins and nucleic acids: their identification, structural characterisation, and functional analysis (including bioinformatics).

Case studies of groundbreaking, modern experiments will be used to illustrate how different biological problems have been dissected at the molecular level.

On successful completion of this course, you will gain the ability to research and critique the scientific literature and to present your findings. You will also develop a working understanding of potential experimental approaches that you can employ when you begin work in professional research laboratories later in your Masters program.ﾠ

This course provides a foundation for an understanding of biochemical methods of experimental protein analysis. There are four core modules in BIOC7040: bioinformatics for proteins and nucleic acids; protein expression and purification; protein characterisation; and protein structural biology. Specialist academics will deliver lectures in each of these modules to provide insights into modern experimental methods for exploring protein function, structure, and biology.ﾠ

course_aims:
• The primary aim of this course is to examine how biological processes are studied at a molecular level, particularly with regard to nucleic acids and proteins. The emphasis is on proteins in important biological roles: their identification, structural characterization, and functional analysis (including bioinformatics), and the concept of conservation of protein fold and function over evolutionary time.

• The course complements BIOC7001.

• Students who complete this course successfully will gain the ability to research the scientific literature, to provide constructive and critical feedback on descriptions of protein research, and to present their findings.

learning_outcomes.description: Correctly explain the theory and practice of modern protein analysis techniques to scientist peers using appropriate visual and oral modes of presentation.
Search protein and literature databases to obtain information about proteins and the experiments used to discover and analyse them.
Read, interpret, and re-present primary literature to peers.
Employ critical analysis of the methods, results, and discussion in primary literature (specifically in studies that address protein structure and function).
Use nucleic acid and protein bioinformatics tools to analyse problems in protein biology, biochemistry, and structure; and describe the use of these tools to an audience.
Be able to propose and justify a workable experimental design for the analysis of a protein structure and/or function.
Provide constructive criticism to a peer on their written and oral presentations and use feedback to make reflective value judgements about the quality of your own written and oral work.

assessment_details.title: Article Review 1
assessment_details.task_description: In this assignment you will write a 3 page critical review the use of an expression and purification strategy (including bioinformatics analysis, where relevant) for the production of a protein in a recent research paper. Supporting workshops will provide guidance on how to choose an appropriate research paper and prepare your review using appropriate online resources. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit through the course Turnitin submission point in Blackboard. Before submitting your assessment item to Turnitin please name your file in the following way. FAMILY NAME_GivenName_StudentID_CourseCode_AssignmentName For example: Bob Smith (ID: 54329876) would name his ABCD1234 essay 1 in the following way. SMITH_Bob_54329876_ABCD1234_Essay1
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension applications must be received no later than 24 hours after the published assessment due date and time.
Read the information contained in the following links carefully before submitting an application for extension to assessment due date.
For guidance on applying for an extension, information is available here .
For the policy relating to extensions, information is available here (Part D).
Information on medical certificates
Please note the University's requirements for medical certificates here .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period). 10% will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved.
In most instances one or more hurdles will apply to your assessment item so you will need to submit it to fulfil the requirements of the course regardless of how late it is and the mark you are likely to be awarded.

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: Students will prepare a research proposal that describes the expression, purification, and characterisation of a protein of interest. The research proposal will take the form of a mock grant application. Specific formatting requirements will be provided on the course blackboard site and guidance on how to prepare the research proposal will be given during the supporting workshops. The proposal should include a summary paragraph, literature review, research hypothesis, clearly stated aims, experimental approach, expected outcomes, anticipated future research directions and a list of references. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 9 (23 Feb - 03 May)
learning_activities.topic: Lectures Please refer to the Course timetable available on the Blackboard site for detailed information.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 13 (02 Mar - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Workshop Please refer to the Course timetable available on the Blackboard site for detailed information. These sessions will provide guidance on assignment content, including practical advice on relevant topics such as strategies for reviewing articles, using citation managers, and best practice for using generative AI tools during assignment preparation. Some workshops will include time for working independently and with peers on exercises that support the assignments.
